Ref: {{ref}}
Pre-Flight Checklist
ESOP Readiness Assessment & Engagement Authorization
Company
{{companyName}}
Seller
{{sellerName}}
Date
{{generatedDate}}
Reference
{{ref}}
§ 01Part 1 — Financial Readiness

The following financial items must be in place before an ESOP transaction can proceed. Each item will be verified during due diligence.

☐3 years of audited or reviewed financial statements — Must be prepared by an independent CPA firm. Internally prepared statements are not sufficient for lender or trustee review.
☐3 years of federal and state tax returns — Including all schedules. Required for QofE normalization and lender underwriting.
☐EBITDA calculation / Quality of Earnings (QofE) — A preliminary QofE must be completed or commissioned. This normalizes owner compensation, one-time expenses, and related-party transactions to establish a defensible earnings baseline.
☐Accounts receivable aging schedule — Current AR aging, bad debt history, and any concentration risk in top 10 customers.
☐Outstanding debt schedule — All loans, lines of credit, equipment leases, and notes payable with balances, rates, maturity dates, and covenants.
☐Capital expenditure history and projections — 3 years of CapEx and forward-looking capital needs. This affects both valuation and post-close cash flow.
§ 02Part 2 — Legal Readiness

Corporate structure and legal standing must be verified before transaction documents are prepared.

☐Current corporate structure — Confirm entity type (LLC, S-Corp, C-Corp), state of formation, and all subsidiaries or affiliated entities.
☐Entity conversion requirements — If not already a C-Corp, identify the conversion path (statutory conversion vs. merger) and state-specific filing requirements.
☐Existing liens and encumbrances — UCC filings, EIDL liens, equipment liens, and any other secured obligations. Provide UCC search results if available.
☐Pending or threatened litigation — Disclose all current, pending, or reasonably anticipated legal actions, regulatory investigations, or disputes.
☐Intellectual property — Identify all trademarks, patents, copyrights, trade secrets, and domain names. Confirm ownership is held by the entity, not individuals.
☐Material contracts — Identify contracts with change-of-control provisions, non-compete clauses, or assignment restrictions that may be triggered by an ESOP transaction.
§ 03Part 3 — Operational Readiness

Operational continuity is critical to post-close success. The following items assess management depth and key-person risk.

☐Management team depth — Identify the top 5-7 leaders by name, title, tenure, and role. Assess whether the team can operate independently of the seller.
☐Key person dependency — Identify any functions, relationships, or knowledge that are concentrated in a single individual (including the seller). Quantify revenue at risk.
☐Succession plan — Is there an existing succession plan for the seller and key personnel? If not, identify the gaps and timeline for development.
☐Customer concentration — What percentage of revenue is derived from the top 10 customers? Any single customer above 15% creates heightened diligence.
☐Vendor concentration — Are there critical suppliers with single-source risk? Identify any vendors representing more than 10% of COGS.
☐Standard operating procedures — Are key processes documented? Assess the institutional knowledge risk if key personnel depart.
§ 04Part 4 — Ownership Readiness

Seller motivation and expectations directly affect deal viability and timeline.

☐Seller motivation — Articulate the primary reason for pursuing an ESOP (retirement, legacy, tax benefit, employee welfare, partial liquidity, other).
☐Desired timeline — Target close date and any external factors driving urgency (health, age, competing offers, market conditions).
☐Post-close role — Seller's desired involvement after close: full exit, advisory role, board seat, employment agreement. Duration and compensation expectations.
☐Family considerations — Are there family members employed in the business? Any intergenerational succession expectations that conflict with the ESOP path?
☐Multiple owners — If there are multiple owners, confirm alignment on the ESOP path. Are all sellers participating, or only some? Minority holder buyout requirements.
☐Valuation expectations — Seller's preliminary valuation range and basis (multiple of EBITDA, revenue, asset-based, other). Will be validated by independent appraisal.
§ 05Part 5 — ESOP-Specific Readiness

ESOP transactions require specialized compliance, trustee, and financing arrangements.

☐ERISA compliance awareness — Seller acknowledges that the ESOP transaction will be governed by ERISA and that an independent trustee must act in the interest of plan participants.
☐Trustee availability — Confirm that a qualified independent ESOP trustee has been identified or will be engaged. Forhemit will coordinate trustee selection.
☐Lender interest — Preliminary lender conversations have been conducted or will be initiated. Seller credit or personal guarantee may be required for senior financing.
☐Employee count and eligibility — Confirm total employee count and anticipated ESOP eligibility. Minimum participation rules under IRC §410 apply.
☐Existing benefit plans — Identify all existing retirement plans (401(k), profit-sharing, pension) and assess compatibility with the ESOP.
☐§1042 eligibility — If the seller intends to elect §1042 tax deferral, confirm C-Corp status, 30%+ ESOP ownership, and qualifying security requirements.
§ 06Part 6 — Commitment & Deposit

By signing this checklist, the seller acknowledges and agrees to the following:

$15,000 Non-Refundable Deposit. The seller will submit a $15,000 non-refundable deposit with the signed checklist. This deposit authorizes Forhemit to begin assembling the transaction team (trustee, ERISA counsel, appraiser, lender). The full amount is credited toward the Engagement Retainer upon execution of the formal Engagement Letter.
A.The information provided in this checklist is true and complete to the best of the seller's knowledge.
B.The seller authorizes Forhemit to begin preliminary work including team assembly, lender outreach, and deal structuring upon receipt of the signed checklist and deposit.
C.The seller understands that the deposit is non-refundable regardless of whether the transaction proceeds to closing.
D.The seller agrees to provide all requested documentation within 10 business days of request.
—Seller Acknowledgment
